problemStatement: How does Phase 4b construct the proof initialization from the raw information derived in Phase 4a?

RULE-1.statement: Phase 4b does not rely on Phase 4a for any structured information. Phase 4a produces raw material (problem statement framing, named Concerns, surfaced observations, surfaced designer directives); Phase 4b structures that raw material into typed proof elements.

RULE-2.statement: Phase 4a is responsible for surfacing the Concerns — the named aspects of the problem statement. Concern surfacing is understanding work.

RULE-3.statement: Phase 4a is responsible for framing the problem statement — designer + agent collaborate during understanding to produce the ratified statement that initializes Phase 4b.

RULE-4.statement: Phase 4a does not do Phase 4b's work. Authoring typed proof elements (Evidence, Rules, Necessary Conditions, Resolve Conditions) is Phase 4b structuring; surfacing concepts in plain language is Phase 4a understanding.

RULE-5.statement: The enter-the-proof gate (Phase 4b initialization) and the exit-the-proof gate (closure) differ by design. Entry is minimal; exit is full. The asymmetry is structural, not accidental.

RULE-6.statement: Element-count discipline lives at exit (closure conditions), not entry (transition gates). Cluster-B does not add boundary integrity gates; closure already gates exit appropriately.

RULE-7.statement: Phase 4b solve depends only on flow-agnostic content. Flow-specific artifacts (Solve Leakage Ledger, active glossary, team-interview Handoff artifact specifics) cannot enter Phase 4b's dependency surface; each flow authors via own means but produces uniform raw material.

RULE-8.statement: Phase 4b's existing solve process is presumed valid and sufficient under cluster-A's element-type addition. Cluster-B confirms by default; refactors only where forced by cluster-A's decisions. Inherited from master-plan RULE-6.

RULE-9.statement: The five existing proof element types (Necessary Condition, Evidence, Rule, Permission, Risk) plus cluster-A's Resolve Condition are immutable. Cluster-B does not propose changes to these. Inherited from master-plan RULE-1 + cluster-A delivery.

EVID-1.statement: The design-large-task skill text describes a three-step Solve Stage Opening sequence: polish/readback/confirm problem statement, initialize_proof, seed initial Evidence + Rules. No step describes Concern enumeration, Concern locking, Resolve Condition drafting, or Resolve Condition ratification.

EVID-2.statement: The proof MCP's checkClosure function enforces ten conditions. Cluster-A added conditions 7-10 (Concerns locked, at least one Concern enumerated, all active Resolve Conditions ratified, per-Concern coverage satisfied). The skill text's Closure Protocol section describes only conditions 1-6.

EVID-3.statement: The proof MCP's state initialization already populates the concerns array, concernsLocked flag, concernCounter, and ratificationLog. The functions addConcern, lockConcerns, and ratifyResolveCondition are exported. RESOLVE_CONDITION is registered in the element type registry. Cluster-A's machinery is fully present; only the skill text describing how to operate it is missing.

EVID-4.statement: The proof MCP's initialize_proof handler accepts only problem_statement and state_file at init time. The cluster-A tools manage_concerns and ratify_resolve_condition are post-init tool calls; they are not part of initialization itself.

EVID-5.statement: The brief template renders Concern IDs as CN-N and Resolve Condition IDs as RC-N, but the proof state generates CERN-N and RCON-N prefixes. Naming inconsistency from a partial rename in commit 8847783; brief template and state code disagree on the canonical ID prefix.

EVID-6.statement: The brief template has five capture-system TODOs adding stable-ID systems for Rules (RE-xx), Permissions (PN-xx), Evidence (EV-xx), Industry Context (IC-xx), Risks (RK-xx) — analogous to the NC-xx system already used for Necessary Conditions. Today only Necessary Conditions and the cluster-A elements (CN, RC) carry stable IDs in the brief output.

EVID-7.statement: The problemfocused flow file declares the Solve Leakage Ledger as Phase-4b seed material, but the skill text's Solve Stage Opening sequence has no consuming step. Declared handoff with no consumer — the dominant industry-reported failure mode for stage handoffs.

EVID-8.statement: The team-interview flow file is the only flow with a complete Proof Seeding mapping table tying Phase-4a output to proof element types (codebase grounding to Evidence with codebase source, philosophy and Exit Criteria to Rules with designer source, ratification dissent to Risks). The other flows have no equivalent mapping.

EVID-9.statement: Audit of 21 StoryDesigner proofs: zero transitioned with Necessary Conditions already present. All seeded 3 to 14 Evidence and 3 to 24 Rules at Round 1 (the existing seed step). Necessary Conditions emerged Round 2 to 4 of Solve work. Sparse final NC counts (2 to 5) closed cleanly; rich counts (17) also closed but took more rounds. Element count did not predict success; scope clarity did. Empirical baseline for the naked-transition / heavy-Round-1 / emergent-NCs / gated-exit pattern.

EVID-10.statement: Industry stage-handoff patterns across Event-B refinement (gluing invariant), NASA V-model (Requirements Verification Matrix), MLIR dialect conversion (ConversionTarget), KAOS goal operationalization (leaf goals + agent assignments), BDD example mapping (rule cards + examples) all converge on the same shape: minimal explicit contract crosses the boundary, receiving stage does heavy structuring in its first pass, downstream verifiers gate exit. Naked-transition + heavy-Round-1 matches the industry-standard pattern.

EVID-11.statement: Designer-direct (this session): zero-NC transition prevention is exit-side (closure conditions), not entry-side (transition gates). The empirical record validates this: every audited proof closed via the closure gates, none required entry-side element-count guards.

NCON-1.statement: The Phase 4b initialization sequence in the skill text must contain explicit, ordered steps that pair each kind of raw Phase 4a material with the proof API call that structures it AND name the responsibility for each step. The pairings: (a) agent-drafted problem statement → designer-approved → initialize_proof. (b) agent-drafted draft Concerns from Phase 4a → designer ratifies, agent surfaces additional → manage_concerns + lockConcerns. (c) agent-translated raw codebase observations → submit_proof_update Evidence (source: codebase). (d) designer-direct restrictions captured during Phase 4a → agent scribes → submit_proof_update Rule (source: designer). (e) designer-direct permissions captured during Phase 4a → agent scribes → submit_proof_update Permission (source: designer). (f) agent-drafted Resolve Conditions anchored to ratified Concerns → designer ratifies sequentially → ratify_resolve_condition. Necessary Conditions and Risks are not part of the boundary structuring — they emerge during per-turn Solve work in Round 2 and beyond as the agent reasons over typed Evidence + Rules.
NCON-1.collapse_test: Without responsibility-named pairings in the skill text, agents either (a) skip steps because they don't know which API matches which raw material, (b) violate source attribution by submitting designer-source Rules without designer authorization, or (c) author RCs before Concerns are ratified. Closure conditions 7-10 fire and refuse with messages the agent can't act on, because the procedural steps to satisfy them weren't documented. Cluster-A's machinery sits unused; the deliverable cluster-B exists to produce becomes hollow.
NCON-1.reasoning_chain: IF Phase 4a produces raw material in distinct categories AND the responsibility Rules (RULE-10 through RULE-18) name agent-drafts/agent-translates/designer-authors/designer-ratifies/agent-derives per category AND the proof MCP exposes a distinct API call per category AND the empirical baseline (EVID-9) shows Round 1 of Solve types this material in one batched operation THEN the skill text must enumerate each pairing as an ordered step naming the raw category, the responsible role, the API call, and the source attribution.
rejected_alternatives: Generic 'structure raw material into proof elements' instruction with no per-category pairing or responsibility naming — too vague, agent invents ordering and authorization rules across sessions.
Single batched call describing all structuring at once — collides with cluster-A's sequential-ratification rule for RCs and with the designer-authors-Rules rule (R12).
Defer per-category mappings to per-flow files — violates flow-independence (R7).
List the API calls without responsibility — invites the agent to author Rules under its own authority, violating R12.

RULE-10.statement: The agent drafts the problem statement; the designer approves the draft. Drafting is agent work (polish, clarify, capture); approval is designer authority. The approved statement is what initializes Phase 4b.

RULE-11.statement: The agent is responsible for translating raw information into Evidence elements. Codebase facts surfaced during Phase 4a become Evidence with source 'codebase' at Phase 4b structuring; industry observations become Evidence with source 'industry' or similar; the typing is agent work.

RULE-12.statement: The designer is responsible for Rules. Restrictions on the design space originate from the designer; the agent surfaces candidates during conversation but only the designer authors a Rule. Source must be 'designer'.

RULE-13.statement: The designer is responsible for Permissions. Relief from a Rule originates from the designer; agent surfaces candidates but only the designer grants a Permission. Source must be 'designer'.

RULE-14.statement: The agent is responsible for Necessary Condition derivation. NCs are reasoning conclusions derived from grounded Evidence + Rules + Permissions; the agent proposes them with grounding chains, reasoning chains, and collapse tests; designer corrects, confirms, or rejects.

RULE-15.statement: Phase 4a is responsible for the draft Concerns. The agent drafts named aspects of the problem statement during understanding work; the draft Concerns travel as raw material into Phase 4b.

RULE-16.statement: Phase 4b ratifies the draft Concerns from Phase 4a, and Phase 4b surfaces additional Concerns for ratification when the structuring work reveals aspects Phase 4a did not draft. Ratification is designer-authority; surfacing additional Concerns is agent work.

RULE-17.statement: Phase 4b is responsible for Resolve Conditions. RCs are authored at Phase 4b only; the agent drafts RC statements anchored to ratified Concerns; the designer ratifies each RC sequentially. RCs do not exist as Phase 4a output.

RULE-18.statement: The agent is responsible for identifying and articulating Risks. Risks are agent-authored hazards attached to specific elements via basis links; designer corrects or accepts. Risks may be surfaced during Phase 4a (held as raw observations) or during Phase 4b (typed as Risk elements).

RULE-19.statement: Cluster-B is concerned with Phase 4b initialization only. The Closure Protocol section, the per-turn Solve flow, and any other Phase 4b internal mechanism beyond initialization are out of scope. Sufficiency confirmation is initialization-side only — the boundary sets up Phase 4b such that the existing closure machinery can gate cleanly, but cluster-B does not design closure documentation.

NCON-2.statement: Raw Phase 4a material at the boundary lives in two channels. Channel one: the agent-drafted, designer-approved problem statement, structurally carried as the initialize_proof parameter. Channel two: agent session memory holding draft Concerns, surfaced codebase observations, surfaced designer directives, and identified Risks — accessible to Phase 4b via continuous session context. The boundary carries channel one explicitly; channel two crosses implicitly via memory.
NCON-2.collapse_test: Without NC-2, NC-1's structuring instructions float unbound — 'translate raw observations into Evidence' refers to nothing structurally defined. Agents may invent boundary artifacts that don't exist (a JSON manifest, a brief section) instead of working from agent memory, the actual carrier. Or agents may discard agent memory at the boundary, treating Phase 4b as starting fresh, and produce sparse Round-1 typing that starves Round-2 NC emergence.
NCON-2.reasoning_chain: IF the boundary stays minimal (R5, R6) AND only the problem statement crosses structurally (RULE-7) AND the agent retains continuous session memory across the Phase 4a → Phase 4b transition (audited baseline EVID-9) AND Phase 4a draft Concerns + observations + directives + Risks live in agent memory at the boundary THEN raw material reaches Phase 4b through two channels (one explicit parameter, one implicit memory) and naming both is necessary so the structuring instructions in NC-1 have defined inputs.
rejected_alternatives: Carry channel two as a structural artifact (a manifest file, a brief section, a serialized state snapshot) — violates the minimal-transition Rule (R5, R6, R7) and the empirical baseline (audit shows agent memory is the actual carrier).

Carry only channel one (problem statement) and re-derive everything in Phase 4b from scratch — contradicts the audit baseline (Round 1 types from Phase 4a discovery, not from re-derivation) and would force Phase 4b to redo Phase 4a's understanding work.

Define a third channel for Concerns specifically (separate API at boundary) — over-specifies; the responsibility Rules (R15, R16) place Concern surfacing in Phase 4a (memory) and ratification in Phase 4b, which channel two already covers.

RULE-20.statement: Ratification of Concerns is part of the proof, not part of initialization. Concerns enter Phase 4b as drafts; through the proof's per-turn work the shape evolves — draft Concerns may collapse into a single root Concern, expand into additional Concerns, or refine under examination of accumulating Evidence and Risks. Resolve Conditions take shape downstream of Concern stabilization.

PERM-1.statement: This sprint is allowed to refactor the Phase 4b process. Cluster-A's design brief stands as historical record; cluster-A's locked vocabulary (Resolve Condition, Concern, Ratification, RESOLVE_CONDITION element type) remains immutable; cluster-A's specific implementation choices (lock-at-opening timing, single-event lockConcerns API, closure-condition-7 fixed semantics, lack of merge/split/revise APIs) are open to refactor where the workflow R20 establishes requires it.
PERM-1.relieves: RULE-19 (cluster-B initialization-only scope) + RULE-8 (master-plan RULE-6 preservation bias for Phase 4b sufficiency)

RULE-21.statement: Cluster-A planning stands as a record. Cluster-A's design brief is historical — not binding for cluster-B's refactor decisions — but its locked vocabulary remains immutable per master-plan RULE-1 inheritance and cluster-A delivery. Cluster-B may revise cluster-A's implementation specifics (API shapes, timing, closure semantics) where R20 (Concern iteration through proof) requires.

NCON-3.statement: Phase 4b's per-turn Solve workflow must support iterative Concern refinement — collapse (merge), expand (split), revise, add — driven by accumulating Evidence and Risk examination. Concerns enter Phase 4b as drafts; per-Concern ratification opens the proof; the shape evolves through per-turn work; lock happens at designer-directed stability, not at opening. Closure condition 7 (Concerns locked) gates exit at the late-Solve event. Five integration constraints govern the iteration: (a) sequential ratification holds at every iteration event — re-ratifications after invalidation walk one RC at a time, no batch; (b) late-lock procrastination gate — skill-level hard prompt three rounds before estimated Solve close, MCP-level closure refusal made discoverable; (c) merge evidence bar — agent-proposed merges require Evidence citation, designer-initiated merges unrestricted; (d) split RC autogeneration — split auto-drafts placeholder RCs for each new Concern, no Concern enters coverage check without anchored coverage; (e) invalidation-impact preview — before merge/split commits, agent surfaces the invalidation count so designer can decline before re-ratification fires.
NCON-3.collapse_test: Without the integration constraints folded into NC-3: designer fatigue on iteration prompts produces lazy ratification, reproducing gestalt approval; late-lock procrastination indefinitely refuses closure and stalls sessions; unlimited merges enable coverage bundling gaming (one bundle satisfies the per-Concern coverage check trivially); empty Concerns after split create coverage holes that closure refuses for reasons agents cannot diagnose; surprise invalidation cascades disengage the designer. The simulation showed each failure mode is real against historic briefs; the constraints are how the API survives.
NCON-3.reasoning_chain: IF Concerns iterate through Phase 4b (R20) AND PERM-1 authorizes the Phase 4b refactor AND the simulation across five historic briefs surfaced four high-severity gaming vectors against the bare API (designer-fatigue gaming, late-lock procrastination, cascade fatigue, stale-ratification storm) AND each vector has a concrete mitigation (sequential ratification, late-lock gate, evidence bar, autogeneration, impact preview) AND cluster-A's NC-05 sequential-ratification rule is load-bearing for the iteration model THEN NC-3 must specify the four operations together with the five integration constraints; bare API alone reproduces cluster-A's gestalt-approval failure mode at scale.
rejected_alternatives: Specify operations only; leave constraints to skill-text prose — operations without discipline reproduce cluster-A's failure modes the master plan was designed to close.
Defer integration constraints to a follow-on cluster — cluster-B owns the refactor per PERM-1; partial delivery violates the permission.
Disallow merge entirely (split/revise/add only) — overcorrects; merge has legitimate cases (orthogonal Concerns merging under unexpected evidence).
Make all iteration designer-only (no agent proposals) — kills the discovery-driven workflow R20 establishes.

Lock Concerns at opening per cluster-A's original spec; iteration via destructive recreation (delete-all + re-add) — loses audit trail and ratification provenance, violates cluster-A's NC-06 cleared-on-revise pattern.

RULE-22.statement: Resolve Conditions map to Concerns only structurally (single problem_anchor field; no NC anchor). The closing argument must walk every active Necessary Condition explicitly, naming the tension that NC imposes on the RC set, and showing how the RC set — under that NC's constraint — resolves the Concerns of the problem statement. Four enforced properties: (a) RC coverage in the walk — every ratified RC appears under at least one NC's constraint role; orphan RCs refuse closure. (b) Tension-naming requirement — closing-argument prose under each NC names the specific tension that NC creates on the RC set, not generic boilerplate. (c) Phantom-NC explicit handling — any NC withdrawn during Solve must be explicitly named in the closing argument with disposition (collapsed-into / superseded-by / no-longer-relevant). (d) Living-document discipline — the closing argument is derived from current proof state and rebuilds whenever Concerns, NCs, or RCs change; not an artifact authored once and frozen.

RISK-1.statement: Cascade fatigue at high RC counts. As Concerns iterate per NC-3 and Resolve Conditions proliferate downstream, designer faces high-frequency re-ratification when Concern merges/splits invalidate cascades of RCs. Even with NC-3's prescribed mitigations (sequential ratification, evidence bar for agent-proposed merges, autogeneration of placeholder RCs on split, invalidation-impact preview), high RC counts may produce ratification fatigue and degrade ratification quality. Hazard increases nonlinearly with RC count. RULE-22's living-document discipline depends on RCs staying current; fatigue-driven stale ratifications would erode the closing-argument's coherence guarantees.

RISK-2.statement: Late-lock procrastination. NC-3 prescribes a late-lock procrastination gate (skill-level prompt three rounds before estimated Solve close, MCP-level closure refusal made discoverable). The gate itself may be insufficient under designer fatigue or uncertainty: a designer who is unsure whether all Concerns have surfaced may indefinitely defer the lock, indefinitely blocking closure. Risk is the prescribed mitigation does not actually prevent the failure mode it names.

RISK-3.statement: Designer fatigue on iteration prompts produces gestalt approval. NC-3's iteration model (collapse/expand/revise/add Concerns through per-turn Solve work) requires frequent designer ratification asks. Cluster-A's documented failure mode was gestalt approval — designer batch-approves rather than per-element scrutiny. NC-3's iteration prompts replicate the same approval-frequency surface the cluster-A redesign was meant to address. Risk is reproducing the failure mode at finer grain.

RISK-4.statement: CN/CERN naming inconsistency between brief template and state code (per EVID-5). Brief renders Concern IDs as CN-N; state code generates CERN-N prefixes. Downstream consumers (architects in design-specify, plan-build authors, code reviewers) may misread brief output or fail to match brief identifiers against state-file identifiers when debugging. Cluster-B does not fix this (out of scope per RULE-19); the inconsistency persists into B.1 and B.2 work and into design-specify hand-offs.

RISK-5.statement: Declared-but-unconsumed flow artifacts (per EVID-7) is the dominant industry-reported failure mode for stage handoffs. Solve Leakage Ledger declared as Phase-4b seed material in the problemfocused flow but skill text has no consuming step. Even after cluster-B.1 wires consumption explicitly via NC-1's responsibility-pairing, future flow additions may declare new artifacts without consumers if no skill-level discipline enforces the consumer-first rule. Risk is recurrence beyond the specific Solve Leakage Ledger case.
